---
title: "Case Studies"
subtitle: "COMP 1150 — Computer Science Concepts, Summer 2026"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
toc: false
---

Welcome! These are twelve short case studies about the people and choices
that shaped computer science. Each one tells a true story. Each one shows
you a small piece of code you can work through by hand. And each one ends
with a real question that people still argue about — so you can join in.

You do not need to read them in order. Start with whatever sounds
interesting.

| # | Case study | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | [Lovelace and Turing: Can a Machine Originate?](cs01_lovelace_turing.qmd) | **Available** |
| 2 | [The Silicon Shield: TSMC, Moore's Law, and the New Chip Wars](cs02_silicon_shield.qmd) | **Available** |
| 3 | [Grace Hopper, Compilers & the Abstraction Bargain](cs03_hopper_compilers.qmd) | **Available** |
| 4 | [The Therac-25: When Control Flow Kills](cs04_therac25.qmd) | **Available** |
| 5 | [How We Represent People: ADTs and the Ethics of Data Modeling](cs05_representing_people.qmd) | **Available** |
| 6 | [Was OOP a Mistake? Alan Kay, Smalltalk & the Backlash](cs06_oop_mistake.qmd) | **Available** |
| 7 | The Halting Problem & the Limits of Machines | *In progress* |
| 8 | Open Source & the Code an AI Learned From — Linux, Git & Copilot | *In progress* |
| 9 | From SQL to NoSQL — Why Google Built Bigtable | *In progress* |
| 10 | Three Companies Own the Internet — ARPANET to the Cloud Oligopoly | *In progress* |
| 11 | The Equifax Breach | *In progress* |
| 12 | COMPAS, Facial Recognition & AI Accountability | *In progress* |

## How to read these

Every case has the same four parts:

- **The Case** — what happened, who was there, and what was at stake.
- **How It Worked** — the tech behind the story, with a short piece of code to trace.
- **The Argument** — the debate the case opened up, with both sides laid out.
- **Discussion Questions** — five questions, none with a single right answer.

Plan on about twenty to thirty minutes to read a case, plus more time
for the code and the questions. There is no answer key. The questions
are meant for talking and arguing about with other people — not for
looking up.
